title: Reimagine focus and contrast for compact legend + list widgets to meet VPAT inputs
preview: Background: During the latest VPAT review we surfaced inconsistent keyboard focus behaviors and a handful of contrast regressions in compact list rows and chart legend items. These components are reused across dashboards (dense tables, compact sidebars, micro-visualizations) and are frequently embedded in third-party dashboards via the SDK. The goal of this ticket is to deliver a small-surface redesign that: 1) standardizes focusable roles and tab order for compact list rows and legend chips, 2) stabilizes visual focus styles that work at compact densities without increasing layout shift, and 3) remaps contrast tokens to ensure >=4.5:1 for primary interactive elements and >=3:1 for supplemental text when density constraints apply.

title: Table folding mode and proportional resize for very wide datasets
preview: Introduce a 'folding' interaction and a proportional column-resize algorithm for the virtualized table component to improve usability on very wide datasets. Folding groups low-priority/overflow columns into a single collapsible pane while keeping critical columns visible; proportional resize smoothly allocates width deltas across non-locked columns respecting min/max constraints and user pinning. This ticket covers design exploration, accessible interactions, animation guidelines, and handoff artifacts for engineering implementation.

title: Adaptive column freezing, fluid resize gestures, and filter affordances for large virtualized tables
preview: Purpose: deliver a cohesive set of UX patterns and interactions for very large virtualized tables that make it easier for users to keep context, manipulate columns, and discover sort & filter controls without regressing performance or accessibility. Scope covers: column freezing (pin left/right), fluid pointer + keyboard resize gestures that respect live virtualization, persistent per-column filter affordances, and per-column density overrides. Does not include server-side filtering backends or query design; those will be separate infra tickets.

title: Traffic Orchestrator: Visual Flow for Multi-Variant Rollouts
preview: Design a compact, scannable visual flow for Redwood Console that lets users create, preview, and manage multi-variant rollouts (canary + A/B combinations) with clear rollback affordances and temporal ramps. The goal is a single-pane orchestration surface that: 1) makes intent explicit (who gets what % of traffic and when), 2) surfaces safety signals and automatic fallback options, 3) enables quick edits and fast rollbacks without navigating between multiple modals, and 4) preserves compatibility with existing runbook/playbook concepts. This ticket covers low-fidelity flow maps, mid-fidelity interactive prototype (Figma + clickable timeline), and a feedback pass with Eng/PM including trade-offs for complexity vs. discoverability.

title: Table Personalization: Keyboard-first Compact Views + Saved Column Sets
preview:
Summary:
Introduce a keyboard-first compact view and saved column-set personalization for the virtualized Table component. This ticket covers the design deliverables and UX decisions required to let power users quickly switch between dense/compact modes, persist column visibility + order as named views, and operate all controls via keyboard and screen-reader-friendly announcements.

Background / Problem:
Our current table has several competing needs: teams want high-density views for large result sets, keyboard-oriented power users expect fast toggles without mouse interaction, and admins want the ability to store frequently-used column configurations as named views. Today these are partly implemented as ad hoc features in product code, but there is no cohesive design system pattern or accessible keyboard-first flow. This leads to inconsistent affordances across products, duplicated engineering effort, and poor discoverability for accessibility users.

Goals:
- Define a single, accessible pattern for compact/dense modes that works with virtualization (no layout jank) and preserves keyboard navigation semantics.
- Design a lightweight "Saved Views" model: users can Save/Load/Delete named column sets (visibility + order + density). Views must be discoverable in the table header and operate entirely via keyboard.
- Provide row skeleton / progressive placeholder states when switching views to avoid perceived flicker during kernel re-layouts.
- Hand off clear tokens and component specs for engineering (visual tokens for compact spacing, focus styles, announcement copy).

Constraints & Non-goals:
- This ticket does not implement backend persistence for cross-device saved views; initial implementation stores views in the browser (localStorage) and exposes an API contract for later persistence.
- We will not change the existing sorting/filtering mental model; saved views capture visible columns + order + density only.

Success metrics:
- 30% reduction in time-to-find columns for power users (measured via in-product telemetry) within 3 months of rollout.
- Accessibility audit passes with NVDA/VoiceOver keyboard walkthroughs for the new controls.
- Engineering reports no >10% CPU/regression in render time when switching views on 10k-row datasets (with virtualization enabled).

Attachments / artifacts:
- Figma exploration (high-level flows + tokens): https://www.figma.com/file/EXAMPLE/Table-Personalization-Explorations
- PRD: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/DS/Table+Personalization+PRD
- Prototype (interactive keyboard-first): https://redwood-internal.example/prototypes/table-saved-views

title: Compact viewport sampler and ARIA summaries for embedded time-series
preview: Problem: Compact time-series thumbnails (list/table rows, sidecar cards) are rendering full-resolution paths and verbose tooltips which increases CPU work, memory churn, and creates noisy screen reader output when many charts are present. We need a design solution that reduces render cost, keeps visual fidelity for trend/peak detection, and produces concise, stable ARIA-friendly summaries that don't flood assistive technology.

title: Avatar border and sparkline visibility in low-light theme
preview: Audit and implement token mapping and component overrides to improve perceived contrast and visual clarity for small surface components (avatar borders, compact controls, sparklines) in the low-light theme. Deliverables: token adjustments, new fallback rules, component-level overrides for Avatar, Sparkline, TableRow, and CompactButton, automated visual tests, and a brief dev handoff document for runtime engineers on mapping rules and theming flags.

title: Refactor onboarding micro-assets for dark theme contrast and glyph clarity
preview:
Goal: Complete an accessibility-first dark-mode pass on all onboarding micro-assets and quickstart screenshots so first-time users (including low-vision and high-contrast needs) can onboard without friction. Scope covers: SVG icon set used across onboarding flows, inline illustrative assets (cards/tiles), CTA/button states in welcome & setup flows, and canonical quickstart product screenshots required for release notes and docs.

Key constraints / targets:
- Contrast targets: meet WCAG AA for body text and UI elements (minimum 4.5:1) and AA for large text/icons where applicable (3:1 allowed for large). Aim for 3:1 minimum for decorative glyphs but prefer 4.5:1 for functional icons.
- Iconography: simplify strokes and increase x-height-equivalent metrics for glyphs rendered at 16px and 24px. Use consistent 1.5px stroke on light, 2px effective dark stroke (via inner-outline technique) to preserve perceived weight on dark backgrounds.
- Color tokens: adhere to tokenized palette (surface-elev-2, neutral-700, neutral-200, accent-500) and introduce dark-mode variants (surface-elev-2-dark: #111217, neutral-200-dark: #cfcfcf) to avoid ad-hoc colors in exported assets.
- Screenshots: produce both light and dark mode captures for every onboarding step used in docs, with the dark captures validated against contrast rules and with captions describing theme state.

Deliverables:
1) Updated icon sprite (SVG) with dark-optimized glyphs and an export pack: sizes [16px, 20px, 24px, 32px] + optimized PNG fallbacks.
2) Redlined spec for stroke widths, padding, and hit target rules for onboarding CTAs and micro-interactions.
3) Set of 8 canonical screenshots (light + dark) for docs & release notes with alt text suggestions.
4) QA checklist and automated screenshot diff configuration (Percy / Chromatic notes).

Design trade-offs & decisions made so far:
- Kept primary brand accent hue in light mode for recognition; in dark mode we tone it down to accent-400-dark to preserve button label contrast (decision based on PM feedback).
- For some illustrative assets with fine gradients, we replaced gradients with solid tonal fills in dark mode to avoid banding and low-contrast edges on retina displays. This increases clarity but slightly reduces stylistic detail.
- Decided against increasing icon sizes globally (requested by Eng) to avoid layout regressions; instead we adjusted stroke and internal spacing which preserves visual balance while improving legibility.

Notes on engineering integration:
- Export names follow tokenized pattern: onboarding/icon/<name>/md-24-dark.svg and onboarding/screens/<flow>-dark@2x.png. Eng requested filename parity with existing asset pipeline; see links below.

Acceptance criteria (high-level):
- All onboarding CTAs and functional icons in dark captures meet at least 4.5:1 contrast vs background, or have documented rationale if falling back to 3:1 for large icons.
- Figma file contains redlines and named tokens; engineering can consume export pack without manual edits.
- QA has a per-screen checklist and automated diffs configured; Percy baseline accepted.

title: Chart layer compositing and offscreen canvas strategy for long-series performance
preview: Goal: Define a design + implementation guide for compositing time-series chart layers using offscreen canvases and a lightweight DOM overlay so that charts with tens of thousands of points remain interactive on mid-range machines. This ticket covers visual layering rules, accessible fallback semantics, interactions with legends/tooltips, and token implications for stroke/fill/hover states. We must provide prescriptive patterns the engineering team can follow (offscreen canvas pooling, ImageBitmap reuse, layer invalidation signals) and ensure ARIA semantics are not lost when rendering to canvas.

title: Legend condensation mode for high-density time-series: SR summarization and DOM reduction
preview:
Problem: Large time-series graphs with dozens to hundreds of series overload the visual legend and the DOM, causing slow render, poor keyboard navigation, and noisy screen-reader output. Existing approaches either virtualize the full list (loses context) or truncate visually but still expose many nodes to assistive tech.

Goal: Design a compact ‘condensation mode’ for legends that reduces DOM nodes, provides semantic summarization for screen readers, preserves quick visual discovery for sighted users, and allows progressive expansion. The pattern should be configurable by product and work across canvas- and SVG-backed charts.

Proposed approach:
- Introduce three presentation tiers: concise summary (0–6 visible legend items + ellipses), grouped micro-rows (category buckets with counts), and full-expansion on demand.
- Render the concise UI as a single semantic container for assistive tech (one live region) that exposes an ordered summary string like: "Showing 6 of 72 series: 3 errors, 2 queues, 1 system". Expand/collapse toggles create/tear DOM nodes lazily.
- Add an algorithm to compute semantic buckets via metadata (series tags) with a deterministic ordering for smallest reflow impact. Allow fallbacks to model-based clustering when metadata absent.
- Visual affordances: stacked color swatches next to compressed labels, hover-to-preview tooltip with lightweight sparkline, and keyboard focusable expand control.
- Performance constraints: keep initial DOM nodes <= 8 for large charts; defer heavy operations (clustering, sparkline rasterization) to requestIdleCallback or a low-priority webworker where available.

Trade-offs and constraints:
- Grouping loses immediate per-series visibility for power users; mitigate with fast expand + keyboard shortcuts.
- SR condensation reduces verbosity but requires high-quality summary generation to avoid information loss. This requires input from product and SRE on acceptable summarization granularity.
- Interactions must be consistent with existing legend behaviors (pinning, toggling) and with console rollout controls, so engineering will provide compatibility shims for legacy props.

Deliverables for design handoff:
- Figma component variants for concise, grouped, and expanded states (with tokens for spacing and touch hit targets).
- Interaction spec (keyboard flows, ARIA live messages, focus management).
- Visual regression checklist and recommended perf budgets.
- Accessibility QA checklist and sample VoiceOver/JAWS narratives for representative charts.

Engineering notes included here to avoid misunderstandings: the team should expose a prop `condenseMode: boolean | \"auto\"` and `summaryResolver(seriesMeta[]) => {buckets, label}`. The design provides the summary UX and a minimal SR string pattern to use by default.

title: Component hierarchy rationalization and constraint preservation for Figma library
preview:
Overview:
This ticket organizes and repairs several systemic issues discovered during the February audit of the Figma UI kit: inconsistent component hierarchies, accidental overrides that break auto-layout constraints, and token mismatches across buttons, tables, and chart components. The goal is to rationalize component nesting, preserve constraints (so consumers do not break), consolidate token aliases, and create a non-destructive publish flow with a clear deprecation schedule for old variants.

Background & motivation:
- Multiple teams reported layout regressions after we published the last library patch: primary buttons lost horizontal padding when consumers resized frames, and table cell templates sometimes clipped content due to nested fixed-size frames.
- Current variant naming is inconsistent (e.g., "Primary/Default" vs "Button / Primary"), which makes programmatic mapping and token sync fragile.
- We need to enable future automation (scripted token swaps and component mappings) so engineering can safely adopt renamed tokens and props without manual fixes in hundreds of consumer files.

Goals / Acceptance criteria (must be met before final publish):
1) Hierarchy rationalization: all button, table row, and chart legend components follow a standardized nesting pattern (root > container > content > icon/text), documented in a single spec page.
2) Constraint preservation: when a consumer resizes an instance, padding and spacing remain stable and no clips occur (verified with the consumer regression test suite on 10 representative files).
3) Variant consolidation and naming taxonomy: create canonical names for states and sizes (e.g., Button / Primary / default | Button / Primary / focused) and provide an alias table mapping old names to new ones.
4) Token mapping: produce a token migration map (old token -> alias token -> canonical token) and a migration playbook for engineering to consume.
5) Safe publish process: publish as a major library update with migration notes, create a compatibility layer (legacy tokens prefixed with legacy_) and a deprecation timeline of 4 weeks.
6) Automated checks: add a Figma plugin/linter rule that flags components that do not use the root>container>content hierarchy or have absolute-positioned content that breaks constraints.

Proposed work items / Steps:
- Audit: run component inventory script and flag instances with absolute positioning or mixed-axis auto-layouts.
- Create a canonical component skeleton file (UI-Kit/CANONICAL-SKELETON) and a spec page describing anchor rules and resizing contracts.
- For each affected component family (buttons, table-rows, chart-legend):
  a) Create a paired copy: "CANONICAL - <component>" where we re-create the component with the standard hierarchy and token references.
  b) Add temporary compatibility variants that map old names to the new canonical variants and expose legacy tokens as aliases.
  c) Update documentation and token map.
- Internal review: run design review with PM and 1x engineer (runtime) to confirm constraints are respected across three breakpoints.
- Consumer regression test: pick 10 consumer files (mix of hosted console pages, example apps, and marketing pages) and validate no visual regressions when replacing instances with canonical ones.
- Publish: create a publisher changelog, include mapping table, and publish under ui-kit-2026.03 with a note to do a follow-up deprecation.

Non-goals / Constraints:
- We will not remove old components in this change; deprecation will start after at least 4 weeks and automation has been rolled out.
- We will not change semantic API props used by engineering in the same release; token aliasing will bridge the gap.

Links & artifacts:
- Canonical skeleton draft (Figma): https://www.figma.com/file/example/Redwood-UIkit-Canonical
- Audit spreadsheet (Drive): https://drive.google.com/example/figma-audit-2026-02
- Migration playbook (Confluence): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/DS/Figma+Migration+Playbook
- Proposed publish changelog (Google Doc): https://docs.google.com/example/ui-kit-2026-03-changes

  a) Create a paired copy: "CANONICAL - <component>" where we re-create the component with the standard hierarchy and token references.
  b) Add temporary compatibility variants that map old names to the new canonical variants and expose legacy tokens as aliases.
  c) Update documentation and token map.
- Internal review: run design review with PM and 1x engineer (runtime) to confirm constraints are respected across three breakpoints.

Non-goals / Constraints:
- We will not remove old components in this change; deprecation will start after at least 4 weeks and automation has been rolled out.
- We will not change semantic API props used by engineering in the same release; token aliasing will bridge the gap.

title: Reduce loading jitter: adaptive spinner, text-preserving states, and theme-aware motion
preview: Problem: Users and telemetry show that button interactions feel unstable when network latency varies: the loading spinner appears too quickly on short waits, causes text shift when replacing label content, and different themes (dark vs light/high-contrast) expose poor contrast or invisible spinners. Additionally, reduced-motion and a11y settings are not respected consistently across icon-only buttons. This ticket defines a design spec for adaptive loading affordances that preserve label width, respect reduced-motion and color tokens, and provide an escape path to cancel long waits. Goals: (1) avoid visual jitter by deferring and crossfading loading indicators rather than snapping; (2) maintain layout stability by reserving label width and optionally overlaying spinner without replacing text; (3) ensure spinner/icon contrast meets WCAG in all themes; (4) add a micro-interaction pattern for icon-only buttons where spinner substitutes with a subtle pulse instead of large motion; (5) document token changes and provide developer acceptance criteria and test cases for regressions.

title: Tenant Admin Role Wizard and Bulk Member Invite UX
preview:
Summary:
Design a guided 'Role Wizard' and an improved bulk-member-invite UX for Tenant Admins as part of RBAC v2. The goal is to make creation, review, and assignment of roles safer and faster while reducing accidental over-permissioning. The experience must integrate permission templates, SSO/SAML group mappings, an audit-preview step, and a simulated permission impact checker tied to audit logs.

Goals:
1) Enable tenant admins to create new roles via a step-by-step wizard that enforces policy guardrails (cannot grant higher privileges than the creator unless explicitly approved).
2) Provide curated role templates (read-only, developer, ops, billing) and allow copying/editing for quick onboarding.
3) Support granular permission toggles with contextual help (what the permission allows, typical risks, linked audit events).
4) Offer a bulk-invite flow that accepts CSV, previews assignments, detects conflicts (existing members, SSO group overlaps), and provides a dry-run that surfaces audit-log implications.
5) Surface SSO/SAML group mapping options inline when members are added via identity provider sync.
6) Include a permission-simulation step that shows which dashboards, routes, and API keys would be affected immediately and historically (backfill considerations).

Acceptance criteria:
- A clickable prototype exists in Figma demonstrating: stepper flow, template selection, granular permission sheet, approval gating, and final audit-preview screen.
- Bulk invite accepts CSV (email, role, metadata) and shows a line-by-line validation screen with inline fixes.
- 'Simulate impact' shows a list of example audit events that would be generated and highlights resources the user will gain access to.
- Validation prevents creating roles that escalate beyond creator scope; modal explains reason and required approvals.
- Accessibility: keyboard navigation, screen-reader labels, color contrast for all states.
- UX copy approved by Security and Legal for audit/logging phrasing.

Design notes and trade-offs:
- We considered an inline modal for role creation but chose a full wizard to let admins review combinatorial effects across resources (projects, models, routes) before committing.
- To reduce friction, templates are prioritized above blank role creation; however, power users can toggle 'advanced mode' to see raw permission bitmatrix.
- We will surface cached KV-policy explanations rather than full policy language to reduce cognitive load; engineers will rely on a separate 'raw policy' view.

Edge cases:
- User tries to assign role to an external SSO group that is not yet synced: show "pending mapping" state and allow invite-by-email fallback.
- CSV contains emails for existing users with conflicting roles: prompt for merge, replace, or skip per row.
- Role with time-bound restrictions (temporary elevated access) needs explicit expiration field; include optional TTL with reminders.

Artifacts and links:
- Figma prototype (current draft): https://www.figma.com/file/placeholder/RBAC-v2-Role-Wizard
- PRD (Confluence): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/Sec/RBAC+v2+Role+Wizard
- Design tokens / component references: https://design.redwood.ai/components/permission-matrix
- Related GH tickets: https://github.com/redwood-inference/platform/pull/3138 (permission simulation API)

Open questions for ENG/PM:
- What is the canonical source of truth for resource scoping (project vs model vs route)? (req'd to populate simulation)
- How should we represent inherited permissions from group-to-user mappings in the preview?
- Rollout strategy: enable wizard for super-admins first or all tenant-admins behind FF?

Next steps:
- Finalize Figma screens with PM by 2026-03-05.
- Usability testing with 3-5 internal tenant-admins (Ops/Finance) 2026-03-08 to 2026-03-11.
- Handoff packet (components, copy, interaction spec) to ENG by 2026-03-15.

title: Reusable spark-band component with ARIA summary and lazy aggregation
preview:
Problem: Several dashboards rely on bespoke sparkline and band components implemented inconsistently across products. This leads to duplicated logic, accessibility variance (different aria summaries), and performance regressions when many small charts render on the same page.

Goal: Design a single, reusable 'spark-band' primitive optimized for dense time-series displays. It should: (1) provide a concise ARIA summary for screen readers, (2) expose a small API for visual and interaction variants (line, area, band, micro-bar), (3) support lazy aggregation/decimation for high-point series, (4) adopt tokens for color/spacing, and (5) include a Canvas-first implementation with an accessible DOM fallback for legacy browsers/screen readers.

title: Contrast-led token rebake and compact UI guidelines
preview:
Background: As Redwood expands dedicated and private deployments, component density and accessibility reports show a recurring set of issues in compact layouts: insufficient contrast on subtle surface ramps, clipped chart axis labels, inconsistent button radii across size variants, and shadow depth leakage at small elevation values. This ticket scopes a focused "rebake" of semantic tokens with a contrast-first lens and a companion compact-mode guidelines doc for components (buttons, tables, charts, form elements).

Goal: Produce an updated token map (color ramps, type scale, spacing/radius, elevation) with clear migration guidance and a small set of component-level adjustments to land in the next console release. Emphasize WCAG AA compliance for normal text and AA/AAA where feasible for UI controls, while preserving the existing visual language and minimizing churn for product surfaces.

Out of scope: Full rebranding, major palette changes, or redesign of core components beyond padding/radius/typography tweaks. Large PRs touching many repos will be coordinated separately with ENG release windows.

title: Miniature Column Summaries, Ambient Density and Coalescing Columns for Virtual Table
preview:
Problem: Our virtualized table works well for wide datasets but in compact/dense modes users lose contextual signals (what columns were trimmed, quick totals, and important cell summaries). Engineering wants a UX that reduces horizontal space pressure without requiring modal previews or persistent column pinning.

Goal: Design a compact-first pattern that preserves discoverability and skim-ability in high-density table views by introducing three complementary concepts: 1) miniature column summaries that condense key metrics into the header, 2) ambient density tokens that harmonize spacing/padding/typography across density modes, and 3) a coalescing behavior that groups low-priority columns into an inline summary cell that can be expanded on demand. The solution must be accessible, implementable within virtualization constraints, and provide clear telemetry hooks.

Proposed solution (high level):
- Miniature summaries: small, single-line summary chips rendered inside headers when density=compact. Chips show a single most-relevant metric (count/avg/state badge) and an affordance to open a flyout with the full column content.
- Ambient density tokens: a small token set (density.ambient.*) to normalize row-gap, header-padding, line-height, and chip sizing so designers and engineers can switch density with a single token map. Tokens must account for keyboard target minimums and be safe for small screens.
- Coalescing columns: algorithm and microinteraction that detects contiguous groups of low-priority columns and collapses them into one coalesced cell at the end of the row group with a summary of values (e.g., "2 hidden — Avg: 12, Status: OK"). Clicking/tapping expands an inline popover with the hidden columns. Coalescing respects column-priority metadata provided by the table API.
- Virtualization constraints: visual affordances must be lightweight (no large DOM of hidden columns). The coalesced summary is a single virtualized cell; expanded content is lazy-hydrated and fetches only visible columns.
- Accessibility: keyboard focus must move into the coalesced summary; screen readers should announce number of hidden columns and allow reading summarized values. ARIA patterns to use: button with aria-expanded on the summary chip, aria-describedby linking to the expanded content region. Ensure reduced-motion respects prefers-reduced-motion.

Deliverables for design handoff:
- Figma spec file with token examples for three density modes (loose, default, compact) and component states for header chips, coalesced summary, and flyout/popover (https://www.figma.com/file/example-mini-summaries).
- Interaction prototype (click/keyboard) showing coalescing threshold changes and expansion behavior (desktop/tablet).
- Accessibility checklist and suggested ARIA attributes, plus example voiceover/screen reader transcripts.
- Implementation notes: CSS token map, expected telemetry events (coalesce_shown, coalesce_expanded, summary_chip_opened), guidance for virtualization teams on lazy hydration and minimal DOM footprint.

Acceptance criteria (must be testable):
1) Compact density shows miniature header summaries for columns marked with summary=true and does not exceed header height baseline.
2) Coalescing triggers when three or more contiguous low-priority columns fall outside container width; coalesced cell displays correct count and aggregated summary text.
3) Expanding the coalesced cell reveals lazy-hydrated rows of the hidden columns; virtualization does not render hidden columns until expanded.
4) Keyboard users can reach the coalesced cell via Tab and expand/collapse it with Enter/Space; screen readers announce "3 columns collapsed: [list summary]".
5) Tokens are documented and a design token PR demonstrates density switch without layout regressions.

Open questions / trade-offs:
- Aggregation rules for different data types (numbers, status chips, date ranges) — need PM/Eng input on server vs client aggregated values.
- Visual density minimums vs touch target guidelines on mobile: choose to prioritize compactness or touchability for phone breakpoints.
- Telemetry sampling: how often should coalesce events be captured without inflating event volume?

Stakeholders: Product (Sofia Morales), Engineering (Runtime Table Team: Rhys Carter), Accessibility (Maya Ibrahim), PM (Oliver Grant).

Links and artifacts:
- Figma: https://www.figma.com/file/example-mini-summaries
- PRD (Confluence): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/DS/Mini-Summaries+and+Coalescing
- Example GH issue for virtualization hooks: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/issues/4122

Timeline: Design drafts and internal review by 2026-03-20, engineering spike for feasibility 2026-03-22 to 2026-03-29, final handoff by 2026-04-01.

title: Consolidate surface tokens and compact variants for dense dataviz kit
preview: Problem: The DataViz surface kit (buttons, table rows, chart legends, small density cards) has grown a high-entropy set of visual-only variants and duplicated surface tokens leading to publish failures, inconsistent runtime spacing, and bloated instance counts. Goal: Reduce variant entropy by collapsing visually redundant variants into token-driven states, normalize token names to the code spec, and add a non-destructive publish pipeline with preflight checks and a targeted instance migration plan. This work should enable predictable library publishes, make auto-layout constraints resilient for responsive breakpoints, and reduce downstream engineering churn when mapping Figma tokens to CSS/Design tokens. Deliverables: 1) Audit spreadsheet of all surface tokens and variants across DataViz libraries. 2) Variant compression plan that documents which visual differences become tokens vs which remain variants. 3) Figma library changes (renamed tokens, archived variants, new alias tokens) and a migration script/recipe for instances. 4) Publish preflight checklist and CI gate to prevent regressions. 5) Rollout plan with canary teams and opt-in migration window.

title: Performance Studio: Per-Model and Per-Route Token Flow + Cost Layering Spec
preview: Goal: Define a compact, scannable ‘Performance Studio’ view that surfaces per-model and per-route token flow, latency distribution, throughput, and cost layers in a single composable canvas. Deliverables: 1) chart specs for Token Stream (waterfall + micro-lanes), Token Rate (sparklines + histogram), Latency Distribution (p99/p95/p50 bands + kernel overlay), Throughput heatmap, and Cost Layering (stacked area that maps batching/quantization/cache savings to monetary units); 2) interaction patterns (time windowing, zoom, trace linkouts, model variant toggle); 3) accessibility and performance constraints; 4) annotated mockups and a Figma prototype for designer/PM/eng review. Scope: Per-route drilldown to show the top 5 model variants serving that route, token-level breakdown (prompt vs. generation vs. system tokens), and a cost attribution view that ties token segments to estimated cloud/GPU cost and Redwood unit cost. We will support two roll-up granularities: 1-minute (default) and 15-second (on-demand sampling). Data sources are telemetry pipelines (route metrics, model execution traces, cost oracle). This spec intentionally prioritizes readability for on-call engineers while retaining the depth needed by platform/product owners.

title: Establish clarity scale and press depth for buttons, tables, and surface shadows
preview:
Problem: Our current token set has grown organically and surface depth (shadows/elevation) semantics vary between components. Buttons use a shallow inset on mobile but a heavier drop on desktop; table row hover/active states are inconsistent in depth and contrast; dense card layouts feel visually ambiguous in compact mode. This creates cognitive friction for engineers implementing components and for product teams tuning density.

Goal: Create a small, well-documented clarity scale and a paired 'press depth' vocabulary that governs button depressions, table row activations, and card elevations. The result should be: 1) a reduced set of elevation tokens with explicit usage guidance, 2) accessible contrast bounds for each surface combination (background + elevation tint), 3) migration guidance and codemods for the component library, and 4) Figma components and CSS variables updated for compact and default density modes.

Scope: color palettes (only where they affect elevation overlays), typography scale implications for perceived depth at small sizes, spacing/radius recommendations when elevation changes (to avoid visual conflicts), and three new elevation tokens (clarity-1, clarity-2, clarity-3) plus two press-depth tokens for interactive compression (press-01, press-02). This ticket covers spec, tokens, Figma updates, implementation notes, and rollout checklist. Implementation and PRs will be split into follow-up engineering tickets.

title: Consolidate interaction shadow layers and type-ramp cadence for high-density components
preview:
Goal: unify shadow/elevation tokens used for interactive controls and tune the typography cadence so high-density surfaces (tables, compact forms, small buttons, and chart legends) remain legible and accessible without increasing vertical rhythm.

Scope:
- Introduce a small set of semantic interaction shadows (shadow.interactive.*) that replace multiple ad-hoc shadows on buttons, inputs, and chips.
- Revisit type ramp: tighten micro-scale steps for compact components and add a readable fallback for <12px usages.
- Adjust spacing/radius aliases for dense tables and compact buttons to reduce visual noise while preserving hit target guidance.
- Update chart token mappings for label contrast at 8px/10px sizes and micro-elevation for overlays/tooltips.

Why now: product teams report multiple visual regressions when switching density modes; engineering has trouble mapping component-only overrides to token names. This work reduces token surface area, clarifies semantics, and provides a safe migration path for component teams.

title: Compact pivot lanes: multi-workspace wayfinding and cost-aware sidebar
preview:
Problem: Customers navigating multiple workspaces and routes in the Redwood Console report difficulty locating per-workspace dashboards, rollouts, and cost breakdowns quickly. Current sidebar consumes too much vertical real-estate for users with many workspaces and offers weak affordances for routing between model-specific dashboards under different billing lenses.

Proposal: Introduce a compact "pivot lanes" pattern in the left rail. Pivot lanes are horizontal micro-rows inside an expandable left-rail group that represent orthogonal navigation dimensions: workspace, environment (hosted/dedicated/private), active cost-lens, and rollout stage. The default collapsed state shows one compact lane summary per dimension (icon + 2-word label + badge). Expanding a lane reveals quick-jump items and contextual filters (eg. model variant, region, allocation pool). The pattern aims to reduce vertical height, improve one-click routing to a per-model dashboard, and surface cost controls inline with navigation.

Scope: design exploratory screens, high-fidelity Figma prototype for desktop (>= 1024px), annotated spec for engineering (interaction states, keyboard shortcuts, ARIA roles), acceptance criteria and rollout checklist for feature flags. Not included: mobile redesigns and deep integration with Dedicated autoscaling UX (follow-up ticket).

title: Token Footprint Lenses & Exportable Pricing Tiles
preview:
Problem statement: Customers want a compact, exportable view that lets platform engineers and finance teams understand token-level cost drivers across routes, roles, and time. Existing mockups are either too dense for quick ops triage or too sparse for chargeback. This ticket designs a set of reusable "lenses" — small focused panels — and a tiling system (pricing tiles) that can be rearranged and exported as CSV/JSON/PDF. The deliverable is a high-fidelity prototype, interaction spec, and annotated export contract for engineering.

Scope:
- Token-level breakdown by model, route, customer tag, and time window.
- Attribution view that ties tokens to request type + prompt template + caching behavior.
- Export formats: CSV for finance ingest, JSON schema for programmatic audits, printable PDF for exec reports.
- Tile library with 6 variants (Summary, Timeline, Hotspots, Attribution Heatmap, Top Prompts, Cost Forecast).
- Compact mode for on-call and expanded mode for billing reviews.

Out of scope for this ticket: full chargeback engine, billing pipeline changes, backend aggregation implementation details (until ENG spikes are complete).

title: Stackable component wrap + layout reflow anomalies on Safari/Firefox mobile — aggregation and prioritized QA
preview:
Problem statement:
Multiple regression reports from QA and customer support indicate that certain 'stackable' components (horizontal token stacks, responsive button groups, compact table rows, and chart legend chips) unexpectedly overflow, reflow, or collapse into a single-line on constrained viewports when used in Safari on iOS and Firefox on Android. The behavior is inconsistent across breakpoints and sometimes linked to momentum scrolling or delayed font fallback. This ticket will centralize findings, record reliable repro steps, propose short-term mitigations, and create engineering tickets for long-term fixes.

Scope:
- Reproduce and classify failures into deterministic and timing-dependent categories.
- Produce minimal test cases (static HTML + component instances) for each category.
- Ship a short-lived CSS patch and a token spacing tweak for the next minor UI Kit release to reduce customer impact.
- Create ENG follow-ups for runtime fixes (layout APIs, prefer-reduced-motion interactions, kerning-aware token truncation).

Non-goals:
- Full refactor of responsive grid system (out of scope for this sprint).

title: Incident Worksheet: Structured Error Triage & Retry Heuristics for Console

This ticket covers initial hi-fi prototype, a 3-user moderated usability test, and a short design review with Eng/PM for feasibility and data needs.

title: GPU Hub Minimap & Autoscale Gestalt — compact controls and triage overlay
preview:
Goal: Design a compact, high-density dashboard module (GPU Hub Minimap) for the Dedicated Capacity console that surfaces pool health, autoscaling posture, and rapid triage controls in a single glance. The module must work as a tiled card in the fleet overview and as a focused overlay for incident response.

Why this exists: Customers operating Dedicated GPU pools need a fast, low-noise way to detect emergent latency/cost regressions and take immediate mitigations (burst enable, drain, fallback route) without switching pages. Current fleet pages are too verbose for on-call flow; this ticket explores a condensed gestalt that preserves actionability.

Primary users: SREs, infra/platform engineers, customer success who perform triage or quick tuning on Dedicated pools.

Design constraints and guiding principles:
- Compactness: fit key signals into a 320x200 card while remaining readable on 1440px+ consoles.
- Action-first: expose 3 immediate actions (burst toggle, scale-to-min/max presets, route-fallback) with confirmations and keyboard shortcuts.
- Signal clarity: show health (green/amber/red), 1h and 24h sparkline for latency and token throughput, and a micro heatmap for request distribution.
- Cost-awareness: include ephemeral cost delta estimate for burst actions (e.g., "+$12 / hr") and token-level cost badge.
- Accessibility: color + pattern encoding for health states, focusable controls, and ARIA labels for screen readers.
- Progressive disclosure: detailed metrics and logs open into full Fleet Insights page or runbook links.

Deliverables for this ticket:
- Low-fidelity wireframes for Hub Minimap (tile + overlay) and two variants of the autoscale control (slider vs. segmented presets).
- High-fidelity prototype in Figma showing micro-interactions: burst ramp animation, quick-confirm modal, and cost delta tooltip.
- Clickable scenario flows for: normal ops glance, on-call triage (latency spike), and scheduled burst for heavy batch jobs.
- Usability test script (5 participants) and success metrics.
- Handoff package: tokens, spacing, component spec, copy guidelines, and a short accessibility checklist.

Acceptance criteria:
1) Designer prototype demonstrates all actions and shows cost delta for burst operations.
2) Usability test: 80% of participants complete triage scenario within 90s without assistance.
3) Frontend dev sign-off on component feasibility (max 2 small behavior compromises).
4) Product approves copy and fallback behavior (canary fallback route must be reversible).

Design notes / non-goals:
- This ticket intentionally scopes out rewriting the whole Fleet Insights page — only a compact card + overlay are in scope.
- Deep perf telemetry correlation (trace waterfall) will link to a separate ENG ticket; we will surface summary metrics only.

text:
Goal: Design a compact, high-density dashboard module (GPU Hub Minimap) for the Dedicated Capacity console that surfaces pool health, autoscaling posture, and rapid triage controls in a single glance. The module must work as a tiled card in the fleet overview and as a focused overlay for incident response.

Why this exists: Customers operating Dedicated GPU pools need a fast, low-noise way to detect emergent latency/cost regressions and take immediate mitigations (burst enable, drain, fallback route) without switching pages. Current fleet pages are too verbose for on-call flow; this ticket explores a condensed gestalt that preserves actionability.

Primary users: SREs, infra/platform engineers, customer success who perform triage or quick tuning on Dedicated pools.
